rows.label: negative
rows.right: He/she/it **is not (isn't / 's not) studying** ... You/we/they **are not (aren't / 're not) studying** ...

sections.title: Use
items: Fixed future arrangements: We **'re meeting** our cousins on Saturday.

sections.title: Be careful
items: We use *be going to* for intentions that are not fully arranged yet. ✗ I **'m becoming** a pilot one day.
We do not normally use the present continuous for predictions. ✗ I think it **'s snowing** tomorrow.

sections.title: Use
items: Predictions and opinions: I think people **will live** longer in the future.
Offers and promises: I **'ll help** you with your homework.
Instant decisions: I'm thirsty — I **'ll get** some water.

sections.title: Be careful
items: Use *shall* for offers and suggestions with *I* and *we*: **Shall** we go now?
We do not usually use *will* for planned arrangements.

rows.label: statement
rows.right: He/she/it **is ('s) going to study** ... You/we/they **are ('re) going to study** ...

rows.label: negative
rows.right: He/she/it **is not (isn't / 's not) going to study** ... You/we/they **are not (aren't / 're not) going to study** ...

rows.label: question
rows.right: **Is** he/she/it **going to study** ...? **Are** you/we/they **going to study** ...?

sections.title: Use
items: Future intentions: She **'s going to learn** Japanese next year.
Predictions based on evidence: Look at those clouds — it **'s going to rain**.

sections.title: Use
items: Timetables and schedules: The train **arrives** at half past six.

formatDefinitions.displayName: Complete with words from the box

exercises.title: A: Key word transformation (future time)
exercises.instructions: Complete each second sentence using the word given, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence. Write between two and five words.
exercises.studentInstruction: Use the keyword to complete the second sentence. Write 2–5 words only.

items.promptSentence: We've arranged to meet Josh at the school café on Monday.
items.targetSentence: We ...... Josh at the school café on Monday.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is a fixed arrangement, so the correct transformation is "are meeting".
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present continuous expresses a fixed arrangement or plan — not will for a diary appointment.
explanationContent.wordCountMistake: Write between 2 and 5 words, including the keyword "MEETING" unchanged. Contractions such as don't count as one word.

wrongOptions.meeting: Do not leave out or alter the keyword "MEETING" — it must appear in your answer.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Will + infinitive is for predictions or promises — a fixed arrangement needs present continuous.
explanationContent.similarExample: Similar: We're meeting the coach at five.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Count your words (2–5) before you check — include "MEETING" unchanged and read the completed second sentence aloud.

items.promptSentence: I think it will rain later this evening.
items.targetSentence: I think it ...... later this evening.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is evidence-based prediction, so the correct transformation is "is going to rain".
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Going to shows a prediction based on present evidence or a prior decision.
explanationContent.wordCountMistake: Write between 2 and 5 words, including the keyword "GOING" unchanged. Contractions such as don't count as one word.

wrongOptions.going: Do not leave out or alter the keyword "GOING" — it must appear in your answer.

explanationContent.commonMistake: The keyword "GOING" must appear exactly — do not change its form unless the task allows it.
explanationContent.similarExample: Similar: Look at those clouds — it's going to rain.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Count your words (2–5) before you check — include "GOING" unchanged and read the completed second sentence aloud.

items.promptSentence: Don't worry — I promise I'll help you with your homework.
items.targetSentence: Don't worry — I ...... you with your homework.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is a promise, so the correct transformation is "will help".
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Will + infinitive expresses a promise, offer, or instant decision.
explanationContent.wordCountMistake: Write between 2 and 5 words, including the keyword "WILL" unchanged. Contractions such as don't count as one word.

wrongOptions.will: Do not leave out or alter the keyword "WILL" — it must appear in your answer.

explanationContent.commonMistake: The keyword "WILL" must appear exactly — do not change its form unless the task allows it.
explanationContent.similarExample: Similar: I'll call you when I arrive.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Count your words (2–5) before you check — include "WILL" unchanged and read the completed second sentence aloud.

items.promptSentence: They've decided to move to a bigger house next year.
items.targetSentence: They ...... to a bigger house next year.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is a decision already made, so the correct transformation is "are moving".
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Use "MOVING" exactly as given and change the grammar around it to preserve the meaning of the first sentence.
explanationContent.wordCountMistake: Write between 2 and 5 words, including the keyword "MOVING" unchanged. Contractions such as don't count as one word.

wrongOptions.moving: Do not leave out or alter the keyword "MOVING" — it must appear in your answer.

explanationContent.commonMistake: The keyword "MOVING" must appear exactly — do not change its form unless the task allows it.
explanationContent.similarExample: Similar pattern: keep "MOVING" and rewrite the grammar to match the prompt meaning.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Count your words (2–5) before you check — include "MOVING" unchanged and read the completed second sentence aloud.

items.promptSentence: She intends to learn Japanese next year.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is an intention, so the correct transformation is "is going to learn".
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Going to shows a prediction based on present evidence or a prior decision.
explanationContent.wordCountMistake: Write between 2 and 5 words, including the keyword "GOING" unchanged. Contractions such as don't count as one word.

wrongOptions.going: Do not leave out or alter the keyword "GOING" — it must appear in your answer.

explanationContent.commonMistake: The keyword "GOING" must appear exactly — do not change its form unless the task allows it.
explanationContent.similarExample: Similar: Look at those clouds — it's going to rain.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Count your words (2–5) before you check — include "GOING" unchanged and read the completed second sentence aloud.

items.promptSentence: Would you like me to carry your bags?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is offering help, so the correct transformation is "Shall I".
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Use "SHALL" exactly as given and change the grammar around it to preserve the meaning of the first sentence.
explanationContent.wordCountMistake: Write between 2 and 5 words, including the keyword "SHALL" unchanged. Contractions such as don't count as one word.

wrongOptions.shall: Do not leave out or alter the keyword "SHALL" — it must appear in your answer.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check that your answer keeps the keyword, matches the word limit, and preserves the meaning of the prompt sentence.
explanationContent.similarExample: Similar pattern: keep "SHALL" and rewrite the grammar to match the prompt meaning.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Count your words (2–5) before you check — include "SHALL" unchanged and read the completed second sentence aloud.

exercises.title: B: will or shall
exercises.instructions: Complete each sentence with a verb from the box. Will and shall are already in the sentences. Use each verb once.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete each sentence with a verb from the box. Use each verb once.

items.sentence: I think it will ...... later this evening.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is a prediction about the weather, so "rain" is the word from the box that fits.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "rain" is the bank word that matches the meaning of the sentence; other chips such as "buy", "call", "help" do not.

wrongOptions.buy: "buy" does not match the meaning or collocation needed in this sentence — "rain" fits better.
wrongOptions.call: "call" does not match the meaning or collocation needed in this sentence — "rain" fits better.
wrongOptions.help: "help" does not match the meaning or collocation needed in this sentence — "rain" fits better.
wrongOptions.meet: "meet" does not match the meaning or collocation needed in this sentence — "rain" fits better.
wrongOptions.move: "move" does not match the meaning or collocation needed in this sentence — "rain" fits better.
wrongOptions.show: "show" does not match the meaning or collocation needed in this sentence — "rain" fits better.
wrongOptions.wait: "wait" does not match the meaning or collocation needed in this sentence — "rain" fits better.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Pick the bank word that matches the sentence meaning first — then check the verb form if will/shall is already in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Will and shall are already in the sentence — the gap only needs the base verb from the box.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: After will/shall, use the base form of the verb from the box — do not add extra endings.

exercises.title: C: be going to
exercises.instructions: Complete using the correct form of be going to and the verbs in brackets. You may have to use some negative forms.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete using the correct form of be going to and the verbs in brackets. You may have to use some negative forms.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is a plan for university, so the correct answer is "'m going to study".

wrongOptions.study: The bare infinitive (study) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Time words like already, yet, since, and for are strong signals for present perfect.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Choose the future form that matches whether the sentence predicts, plans, or arranges.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is a plan for this weekend, so the correct answer is "are going to paint".

wrongOptions.paint: The bare infinitive (paint) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.grammarFocus: Choose the future form that matches whether the sentence predicts, plans, or arranges.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Choose the future form that matches whether the sentence predicts, plans, or arranges.

exercises.exerciseTypeName: E: Circle the correct word or phrase
exercises.title: E: Circle the correct word or phrase
exercises.instructions: Circle the correct word or phrase.
exercises.studentInstruction: Tap the correct word or phrase.

items.originalSentence: I think Ella **will become / is becoming** a great doctor one day.
items.completedSentence: I think Ella will become a great doctor one day.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "will become" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present simple describes habits, routines and general facts. Present continuous describes actions happening now or temporary situations.

wrongOptions.is becoming: "is becoming" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("a great doctor one day.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: Look for time clues — frequency words (very often, usually) point to present simple; right now and at the moment point to present continuous.

optionContrast.is becoming: "is becoming" doesn't fit here → "will become" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("a great doctor one day.").

items.originalSentence: We **are meeting / will meet** our teacher after class today.
items.completedSentence: We are meeting our teacher after class today.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "are meeting" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Use present continuous with time clues like right now, at the moment and this month.

wrongOptions.will meet: "will meet" does not match the present continuous time clue in this sentence.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Look for time clues — frequency words (very often, usually) point to present simple; right now and at the moment point to present continuous.

optionContrast.will meet: "will meet" doesn't fit here → "are meeting" does, because the time clue signals the present continuous.

items.originalSentence: What **are you going to wear / do you wear** to the party tonight?
items.completedSentence: What are you going to wear to the party tonight?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "are you going to wear" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Use present continuous with time clues like right now, at the moment and this month.

wrongOptions.do you wear: "do you wear" describes a habit or general fact, but the time clue signals an action happening now.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Look for time clues — frequency words (very often, usually) point to present simple; right now and at the moment point to present continuous.

optionContrast.do you wear: "do you wear" doesn't fit here → "are you going to wear" does, because the time clue signals the present continuous.

items.originalSentence: **Will you help / Shall you help** me carry these bags?
items.completedSentence: Will you help me carry these bags?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "Will you help" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "Will you help" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "Shall you help" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.Shall you help: "Shall you help" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("me carry these bags?").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

optionContrast.Shall you help: "Shall you help" doesn't fit here → "Will you help" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("me carry these bags?").

items.originalSentence: My brother **is going to study / will study** medicine at university.
items.completedSentence: My brother is going to study medicine at university.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "is going to study" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present simple describes habits, routines and general facts. Present continuous describes actions happening now or temporary situations.

wrongOptions.will study: "will study" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("medicine at university.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: Look for time clues — frequency words (very often, usually) point to present simple; right now and at the moment point to present continuous.

optionContrast.will study: "will study" doesn't fit here → "is going to study" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("medicine at university.").

items.originalSentence: We **are having / will have** a science test tomorrow morning.
items.completedSentence: We are having a science test tomorrow morning.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "are having" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present simple describes habits, routines and general facts. Present continuous describes actions happening now or temporary situations.

wrongOptions.will have: "will have" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("a science test tomorrow morning.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: Look for time clues — frequency words (very often, usually) point to present simple; right now and at the moment point to present continuous.

optionContrast.will have: "will have" doesn't fit here → "are having" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("a science test tomorrow morning.").

items.originalSentence: I'm sure you **will enjoy / are enjoying** the concert.
items.completedSentence: I'm sure you will enjoy the concert.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "will enjoy" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present simple describes habits, routines and general facts. Present continuous describes actions happening now or temporary situations.

wrongOptions.are enjoying: "are enjoying" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("the concert.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: Look for time clues — frequency words (very often, usually) point to present simple; right now and at the moment point to present continuous.

optionContrast.are enjoying: "are enjoying" doesn't fit here → "will enjoy" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("the concert.").

items.originalSentence: **Are they travelling / Do they travel** to Italy next month?
items.completedSentence: Are they travelling to Italy next month?

explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present simple describes habits, routines and general facts. Present continuous describes actions happening now or temporary situations.
